# Principles of Sticky Ideas

**Principle 1: Simple — Find the core. Southwest: THE low-fare airline.**

**Principle 2: Unexpected — Surprise grabs attention. Curiosity keeps it.**

**Principle 3: Concrete — Make ideas tangible. Vivid details stick.**

**Case: The Brown Eye Experiment. A teacher divided her class by eye color. Abstract prejudice became concrete.**

**Case: The Nuclear BBs. "Warheads are like BBs across the US" makes statistics concrete.**

**Case: The Commander's Intent. Every mission needs a core sentence.**
---

*Generated by [Heardly App](https://www.heard.ly) — turning books into knowledge you can Listen and Execute.*

*Generated by [Heardly App](https://www.heard.ly) — turning books into knowledge you can Listen and Execute.*

**Case: The Sinatra Test. New York: if you can make it there, you can make it anywhere.**
**Case: The Tylenol Recall. J&J pulled all Tylenol from shelves after poisonings. It was simple, credible, and concrete.**
**Case: The Palm Pilot. Sony's first e-reader failed because it was not simple enough.**
**Case: The Aesop Fable. The tortoise and the hare is a simple story that sticks after 2,500 years.**
**Case: The Multiple Hooks. Velcro works because it has many hooks. Ideas work the same way.**
**Case: The Gaps. Creating a curiosity gap: you know the beginning. You want to know the end.**
**Case: The Schema. We understand new ideas by connecting them to existing schemas.**
**Case: The Core Message. Everything else is secondary. The core is what sticks.**
